items.evidence_text:
Client: Hey Alex. Post-divorce, I just want to make sure I’m not missing something obvious—like taxes or retirement. I’m doing fine, I think, but I want a sanity check.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll keep it practical. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow—what’s your total gross income for the year, all sources combined?
Client: All-in, my household gross income is 157400 annually.
Advisor: Got it. And that total is coming from a few buckets—salary, bonus, and then investment income, right?
Client: Yeah. Salary is 51000 a year, bonus is 15700, and then interest and dividends are split—40700 and 50000 annually.

Client: Yeah. Salary is 51000 a year, bonus is 15700, and then interest and dividends are split—40700 and 50000 annually.
Advisor: Okay—thanks. On the spending side, what do your monthly expenses usually run, roughly?
Client: Around seven grand a month—actually the number I track is 7100 per month.
Advisor: Perfect. And debt—do you have any monthly debt payments right now? Mortgage, car, credit cards, anything like that?
Client: No. Monthly debt payments total is 0.

Client: Hey Alex. Post-divorce, I just want to make sure I’m not missing something obvious—like taxes or retirement. I’m doing fine, I think, but I want a sanity check.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll keep it practical. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow—what’s your total gross income for the year, all sources combined?
Client: All-in, my household gross income is 157400 annually.
Advisor: Got it. And that total is coming from a few buckets—salary, bonus, and then investment income, right?
Client: Yeah. Salary is 51000 a year, bonus is 15700, and then interest and dividends are split—40700 and 50000 annually.

Client: No. Monthly debt payments total is 0.
Advisor: Nice and clean. Let’s shift to what you’ve built up—about how much do you have in investable assets, not counting personal property?
Client: Investable assets total is 303200.
Advisor: One sec—just noting that. Can you walk me through what makes up that 303200? Where is it held and what kind of accounts are we talking about?
Client: Sure. The biggest is a taxable account on an advisor platform—204200.

Client: Plus a small private markets position at a brokerage—1000.
Advisor: Thanks. And you mentioned you own your home—what are you using as the current value for your primary residence?
Client: 387350 is the value I’m using for the primary residence.
Advisor: Great. And just to confirm on liabilities overall—you said no loans outstanding at all, correct?
Client: Correct, loans outstanding are 0.

Client: Yeah. Salary is 51000 a year, bonus is 15700, and then interest and dividends are split—40700 and 50000 annually.

Client: Hey Alex. Post-divorce, I just want to make sure I’m not missing something obvious—like taxes or retirement. I’m doing fine, I think, but I want a sanity check.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll keep it practical. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow—what’s your total gross income for the year, all sources combined?
Client: All-in, my household gross income is 157400 annually.
Advisor: Got it. And that total is coming from a few buckets—salary, bonus, and then investment income, right?
Client: Yeah. Salary is 51000 a year, bonus is 15700, and then interest and dividends are split—40700 and 50000 annually.

Advisor: Okay. Sometimes people mean “no payment” but there’s still a small balance—just checking. So the mortgage balance is literally zero.
Client: Yeah, literally zero. It’s paid off.
Advisor: Great. Any other loans anywhere—car loan, personal loan, business loan in your name—anything that would count as loans outstanding?
Client: No—loans outstanding are 0.
Advisor: And non-mortgage debt—credit cards you carry month to month, lines of credit, anything like that—what’s outstanding right now?

Client: Hey Alex. Post-divorce, I just want to make sure I’m not missing something obvious—like taxes or retirement. I’m doing fine, I think, but I want a sanity check.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll keep it practical. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow—what’s your total gross income for the year, all sources combined?
Client: All-in, my household gross income is 157400 annually.
Advisor: Got it. And that total is coming from a few buckets—salary, bonus, and then investment income, right?
Client: Yeah. Salary is 51000 a year, bonus is 15700, and then interest and dividends are split—40700 and 50000 annually.

Advisor: Got it. When you file, what tax bracket are you generally landing in these days—ballpark is fine.
Client: I’m in the 35% tax bracket band.
Advisor: Okay—35%. And just so I’m clear on how we’re working together: are you looking for this to be through an RIA, like a registered investment advisor relationship, versus a brokerage-only setup?
Client: Yes—advisory channel is RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Perfect. And just to connect the dots with what you already laid out: you’ve got gross income 157400 a year, expenses 7100 a month, and no loans outstanding—0—so the tax planning is really going to be about the income mix and where the accounts sit.

Client: Number of dependents is 0.
Advisor: Got it. When you file, what tax bracket are you generally landing in these days—ballpark is fine.
Client: I’m in the 35% tax bracket band.
Advisor: Okay—35%. And just so I’m clear on how we’re working together: are you looking for this to be through an RIA, like a registered investment advisor relationship, versus a brokerage-only setup?
Client: Yes—advisory channel is RIA (registered investment advisor).

Advisor: Hi Daniel—good to meet you. I’m Alex. Before we get into numbers, what made you decide to come in now?
Client: Hey Alex. Post-divorce, I just want to make sure I’m not missing something obvious—like taxes or retirement. I’m doing fine, I think, but I want a sanity check.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll keep it practical. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow—what’s your total gross income for the year, all sources combined?
Client: All-in, my household gross income is 157400 annually.

Advisor: Totally. And protections-wise, you said no protection policies in place right now—no life or disability—still accurate?
Client: Still accurate—no protection policies.
Advisor: Great. Next meeting we’ll map goals and then talk through what “moderate risk” means for you in practice, especially with that taxable account and the retirement account you mentioned.
Client: Sounds good—just keep it tight and actionable.
Advisor: Perfect, Daniel. Since you want this tight—two quick household basics I don’t want to assume. First, you’re planning and filing here in the US, right?

Client: Hey Alex. Post-divorce, I just want to make sure I’m not missing something obvious—like taxes or retirement. I’m doing fine, I think, but I want a sanity check.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll keep it practical. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow—what’s your total gross income for the year, all sources combined?
Client: All-in, my household gross income is 157400 annually.
Advisor: Got it. And that total is coming from a few buckets—salary, bonus, and then investment income, right?
Client: Yeah. Salary is 51000 a year, bonus is 15700, and then interest and dividends are split—40700 and 50000 annually.

Advisor: Got it. And household-wise, is it just you as an adult in the household right now, or anyone else living with you as an adult?
Client: It’s just me—number of adults is 1.
Advisor: Okay. And marital status—just confirming after the divorce—are you currently divorced, single, remarried… where does that land today?
Client: Marital status is divorced.
Advisor: Thanks. Now, you mentioned earlier “moderate risk,” and we’ll unpack it next time, but I want to capture it cleanly today—how would you label your overall risk tolerance?

Client: Hey Alex. Post-divorce, I just want to make sure I’m not missing something obvious—like taxes or retirement. I’m doing fine, I think, but I want a sanity check.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll keep it practical. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow—what’s your total gross income for the year, all sources combined?
Client: All-in, my household gross income is 157400 annually.
Advisor: Got it. And that total is coming from a few buckets—salary, bonus, and then investment income, right?
Client: Yeah. Salary is 51000 a year, bonus is 15700, and then interest and dividends are split—40700 and 50000 annually.

Advisor: Okay. Last thing for today, Daniel—do you want me to send a short follow-up list of what we’ll cover next time: goals, liquidity target, and then a risk plan around that taxable account?
Client: Yes, that’d be helpful—keep it bullet points.
Advisor: Perfect—I'll send that bullet-point follow-up. Before we wrap, Daniel, I want to make sure I’ve got your basic profile right: are you currently employed, or self-employed, or something else?
Client: I’m employed.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I label things correctly in my notes—between you and anyone else, you’re the primary person we’re planning around, right?

Client: Hey Alex. Post-divorce, I just want to make sure I’m not missing something obvious—like taxes or retirement. I’m doing fine, I think, but I want a sanity check.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll keep it practical. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow—what’s your total gross income for the year, all sources combined?

Client: Yeah. Salary is 51000 a year, bonus is 15700, and then interest and dividends are split—40700 and 50000 annually.

Advisor: Okay.
Client: Plus a small private markets position at a brokerage—1000.
Advisor: Thanks. And you mentioned you own your home—what are you using as the current value for your primary residence?
Client: 387350 is the value I’m using for the primary residence.
Advisor: Great. And just to confirm on liabilities overall—you said no loans outstanding at all, correct?

Client: Right—gross before taxes.
Advisor: Perfect. Daniel, last couple things so I’m not guessing—when you say you’re employed, what’s your role day-to-day? Are you an employee somewhere, or are you running your own shop?
Client: I’m a business owner.
Advisor: Got it—business owner. And is that the main thing driving the salary and the bonus we talked about earlier?
Client: Yeah, that’s the main thing.

Client: All-in, my household gross income is 157400 annually.
Advisor: Got it. And that total is coming from a few buckets—salary, bonus, and then investment income, right?
Client: Yeah. Salary is 51000 a year, bonus is 15700, and then interest and dividends are split—40700 and 50000 annually.
Advisor: Okay—thanks. On the spending side, what do your monthly expenses usually run, roughly?
Client: Around seven grand a month—actually the number I track is 7100 per month.

Advisor: Great. When you say moderate, is that more like “I can handle normal ups and downs,” but you don’t want anything that could blow up your taxable account?
Client: Yeah, exactly—normal swings are fine, I just don’t want a big drawdown that forces me to sell at a bad time.
Advisor: Makes sense. And just to connect it back to what we already covered—your income mix is that weekly salary and the interest and dividends, plus the ad hoc bonus, and then assets are that taxable account on the advisor platform, the retirement account and bank-type account through the insurance company, the small private markets position at the brokerage, and your primary residence—still all accurate?
Client: Yep, all accurate.
Advisor: And liabilities are still clean—no loans outstanding and no carried credit card balances—still zero across the board?

Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll keep it practical. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow—what’s your total gross income for the year, all sources combined?

Client: Yep, I’m Client 1—1.
Advisor: Great. Now, income—earlier we talked about the mix, but I want to confirm the total gross annual number. What are you using as your gross income for the year?
